item.title: The AI Your Kids Are Already Using, Without Knowing It's AI
item.description: Before your child ever opens ChatGPT, they've already been shaped by AI: in their TikTok feed, their Snapchat filters, their game recommendations, and the autocomplete on their phone. Here's what that actually means.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: Do It By Hand First: A Method for Keeping Critical Thinking Alive in the Age of AI
item.description: There's a habit I've used for twenty years to learn hard things at work: solve it by hand, write down how, script the parts that repeat, and only automate once I truly understand it. It turns out that same order, do the work first and hand it off last, lines up with a lot of what we know about how durable understanding forms, and with the kind of thinking AI makes it dangerously easy to skip. Here's the method, the research behind it, and how to use it with your kids.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily
item.category: parenting in the age of AI

item.title: Is Using AI Cheating? What Schools Actually Expect (And How to Find Out)
item.description: "Is AI cheating?" is the wrong question. The right question is what your child's specific school expects for each specific assignment — and how to find out before something goes wrong.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: When Your Child's Best Friend Is an AI: Companions, Chatbots, and Emotional Dependency
item.description: Apps like Character.ai are designed to feel like relationships. For some children, that's genuinely useful. For others, it becomes a problem. Here's what parents need to know.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: I Asked Claude to Build a Real Tool in an Evening — Here's Exactly How, Including What Went Wrong
item.description: You keep hearing that "AI can build software now." That's true, and it's also more complicated than the headline. So I asked Claude to build a real, working tool — a photo-blur scanner — and it did. I didn't write a line of it. I kept every prompt, then had Claude analyze its own build. Here's the whole thing: the prompts I typed, the decisions I made, the night it quietly accomplished nothing, and what it cost.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: What If Your Child Is the One Making Deepfakes
item.description: Post 9 covered what to do if your child is a victim of deepfakes. This post covers the harder conversation — what to do if your child is the one creating them.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: Try It Yourself: What Twelve Posts About AI Taught Me About Parenting in the Age of AI
item.description: Twelve posts. Twelve weeks of practical AI guidance for parents. Here's what the series covered, the challenges it raised, and the through-lines that connect it all.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: Part 12 - Keeping Up When AI Changes Every Month: Five Resources Every Parent Should Bookmark
item.description: AI changes fast. This final post gives you five resources to bookmark so you can stay current without spending more time on it than you have.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: Part 11 - Writing Your Family AI Agreement: A Step-by-Step Guide
item.description: After ten posts building your own understanding of AI, you're ready to have the family conversation. Here's how to write an agreement your children will actually follow.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: Part 10 - Recognizing Bias in AI Answers: A Skill Every Parent Can Learn
item.description: AI produces the most common answer, not the most accurate one — and "most common" reflects whose voices dominated its training data. Here's how to recognize bias and teach your children to do the same.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: Part 9 - Deepfakes Are Real: What Parents Need to See to Understand
item.description: Deepfakes — AI-generated fake images, video, and audio — are already being used to harm people. Here's what parents need to understand, and what to do if something goes wrong.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: Part 8 - AI for Busy, Overwhelmed, or Differently-Abled Parents
item.description: AI's accessibility features aren't just for students. Here are four concrete ways AI can help parents with translation, summarization, planning, and drafting.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: Part 7 - School AI vs. Home AI: What Every VSB Parent Should Understand
item.description: The school version of Copilot and the home version share a name but operate under very different rules. Here's what VSB parents need to know.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: Part 6 - Using AI as Your Personal Research Assistant
item.description: AI is genuinely useful for learning tasks: summarizing documents, explaining concepts, and generating questions. Here are three concrete things to try this week.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: Part 5 - How to Spot AI Mistakes Before Your Kids Don't
item.description: AI mistakes look identical to correct answers. This post gives you a simple routine for catching errors — one you can use yourself and teach to your children.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: Part 4 - Your Privacy Checklist: What Never to Share With AI
item.description: Before you spend much time with AI tools, there's one thing you need to understand: what you type doesn't always stay private. Here's what to know and what to check.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: Part 3 - ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity: A Plain-Language Comparison for Parents
item.description: A plain-language guide to the five AI tools your children are most likely to encounter — what each one does, who made it, and which one to try first.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: Want to Build an AI Guide for Parents in Your Province or Territory? Here's Where to Start.
item.description: A Parent's Guide to AI was written for BC. Most of it applies across Canada — but school policies, privacy laws, and local resources vary by province. Here's a practical roadmap for building a version specific to where you live.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: Part 2 - What AI Actually Is (And Why That Matters for Parents)
item.description: AI works like a very sophisticated autocomplete — it predicts words, it doesn't think. Understanding this changes how you respond when your child brings home AI-assisted work.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: Part 1 - Before You Talk to Your Kids About AI, Try It Yourself
item.description: The best way to guide your children through the AI era is to try these tools yourself first. Here's how to get started in five minutes.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily

item.title: Introducing A Parent's Guide to Artificial Intelligence
item.description: Introducing A Parent's Guide to Artificial Intelligence — a free, plain-language resource for BC parents navigating AI in schools and at home.
item.dc:creator: Yvan Boily
